slides.title: Python Does Math Too
slides.body: Python has **seven arithmetic operators** — the four you know from school, plus three powerful extras. Master these and you can make Python calculate almost anything.
slides.tip: Python arithmetic works directly in the REPL — try typing `2 + 2` and pressing Enter.

slides.title: Division Always Returns a Float
slides.body: The `/` operator **always** produces a decimal result, even when the answer is a whole number. Use `//` (floor division) when you only want the whole-number part.
slides.code:
print(10 / 2)    # always a float
print(10 // 3)   # whole part only
slides.tip: Think of `//` as 'how many times does it fit completely?' — like sharing cookies with no crumbs.

slides.title: The Remainder: % (Modulo)
slides.body: The `%` operator gives the **leftover** after division. It's one of the most useful operators in Python — perfect for even/odd checks and wrapping values.
slides.code:
print(10 % 3)    # 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1
print(42 % 2)    # 0 means even
print(7 % 2)     # 1 means odd
slides.tip: Modulo is like a clock — after 12, it wraps back. `14 % 12` gives `2`, just like 14:00 is 2 o'clock.

slides.title: Powers and Square Roots: **
slides.body: Use `**` to raise a number to a power. Fractional powers work too — `x ** 0.5` is the square root of `x`, no special function needed.
slides.code:
print(2 ** 8)    # 2 to the power of 8
print(9 ** 0.5)  # square root of 9
slides.tip: `**` chains right-to-left: `2 ** 3 ** 2` means `2 ** (3**2)` = 512, not 64. Use parentheses to be safe.

slides.title: You've Got the Operators!
slides.body: You now know all seven Python arithmetic operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `//`, `%`, and `**`. Remember: `/` always returns a `float`, `//` gives the whole part, and `%` gives the remainder.
slides.tip: When in doubt about order of operations, just add parentheses — they always win and make your code easier to read.
